but in the third month we spotted a significant enlargement of the mass so we decided to go over with surgery after complete clinical reassessment that didn't show any sign of distance disease progression we use the SI platform with a three-arm technique at the beginning we can see some pleural adhesions probably due to the immunotherapy
After the docking, we start the procedure by breaking the pleural adhesions.
Then we find more adhesion at the apex of the left lung.
On the anterior portion of the superior lobe, we found the thicker adhesions
After almost 20 minutes freeing the lung from all pleural adhesions, we started the lobectomy by freeing the inferior pulmonary ligament and harvesting the lymph nodes from chain 9.
Then we retract the lung anteriorly to dissect the hilum posterior.
This way we can see the inferior pulmonary vein
and the bronchus, removing the lymph nodes from station 7.
The next step is to free the posterior portion of the artery.
In this case, it was very difficult due to lymph nodes and the effect of the immunotherapy in the tissue, which was very attached to the artery.
but careful dissection with bipolar, we can manage to remove the lymph node, freeing almost completely the posterior portion of the artery.
The superior portion of the pulmonary artery was hard to reach due to the size of the tumor plus the adhesions from immunotherapy.
We tried gentle dissection with bipolar, but this strategy has limited success at this point.
So we decided to leave it and try another strategy.
We try to expose the artery by dividing the fissure with bipolar.
But as you can see in the video, this strategy was also not appropriate due to tissue inflammation, which leads to technical difficulty plus constant bleeding.
After several minutes on the fissure, we decided to change the strategy.
We tracked the lung posteriorly to dissect the hyaline from the front.
We decided to go like a fissure lobectomy.
Vein, artery, bronchus and fissure at last.
The anterior portion of the hilum was difficult to dissect due to adhesions, but not as difficult as the fissure of the superior portion.
So we can manage to expose and dissect completely the superior pulmonary vein.
Between the artery and the superior pulmonary vein, we also find a lot of lymph nodes that have to be completely removed in order to expose the structures.
Here, after the successful removal of the lymph nodes, we can finally have a good exposure of the first thrombus of the artery.
After complete dissection of the first truncus, we divided using endo stapler with vascular load.
The next step was to divide the upper pulmonary vein also with vascular load.
After the division of the first arterial truncus and superior pulmonary vein, we finally have a good exposure of the bronchus.
So we start to dissect the upper left bronchus.
This was one of the hardest parts of the procedure due to the difficulty of dissection with many lymph nodes and a very inflamed tissue around the bronchus.
But the dissection with bipolar was very precise, so we can manage to take out the lymph nodes to separate the adhesion and free the bronchus from the artery.
Proceeding with careful dissection and changing the exposure, we can manage to take out many lymph nodes and then go around the bronchus.
After almost 30 minutes of hard work on the bronchus, we can finally encircle it with the loop, which helps us a lot to pass the endostapler to divide the bronchus.
After the division of the upper left bronchus, we encountered many lymph nodes blocking the view of the artery.
Once again, we have to proceed with a careful dissection, taking carefully all the lymph nodes to completely expose the artery.
With the complete exposure of the artery, we start to work on the remaining upper arterial branches.
Once again, with a vessel loop helping, we could divide the final arterial branches.
This way, we safely complete the division of the vascular branches and the bronchus from the upper left lobe.
But, we find more lymph nodes and more adhesions and we have to freely before we can complete the lobectomy with the division of the fissure.
As the last step, we complete the fissure with endo staplers from the front to the back.
We have moved the lung back and forth several times to ensure that the endo stapler was placed in the correct position.
We advance load by load, always ensuring in both sides that the endo stapler was in the correct position.
Since the tumor was close to the fissure, we advance with extra caution on the endo-stapler position.
We use four loads to completely divide the fissure.
By the time we separate the superior lobe from the inferior lobe, we check that all tumor remain in the superior lobe.
This is the final view of the bronchus and the artery before the specimen removal.
